_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Simpson County, Kentucky. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the Kentucky-Tennessee border county — formed 1819 from Allen, Logan, and Warren counties, with Franklin as county seat. Simpson County sits on the Nashville pike and the L&N Railroad, making it a corridor county where families crossed between Kentucky and Tennessee. Records are well-preserved; no major courthouse fires.

current_courthouse.location: Franklin, KY — 101 East Cedar Street (Simpson County Judicial Center)

previous_courthouses.location: Franklin — original frame courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county matured. Records transferred intact.

previous_courthouses.location: Franklin — brick courthouse on the same site
previous_courthouses.fate: Served through the Civil War without major damage. Replaced in 1902.

previous_courthouses.location: Franklin — third courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Served eighty years. Records well-preserved. Replaced by the current judicial center.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Simpson County's courthouse has not experienced a significant fire or destructive event. Despite guerrilla activity during the Civil War and the county's location on the Nashville pike — a strategic corridor for both armies — the courthouse and its records survived intact. Records run continuously from the county's 1819 formation. Simpson County's border location means that families frequently appear in both Kentucky and Tennessee records, and cross-referencing with Robertson and Sumner counties in Tennessee is often necessary.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Simpson County's intact records make it one of the easiest Pennyroyal counties for genealogical research from 1819 forward. If {ancestor_name}'s records are missing, the likeliest explanation is that the family was actually in Tennessee — Robertson or Sumner County — where the state line cuts through the same community.
record_losses.source: Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Kentucky County Records. Frankfort, 1937-1942.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Simpson County deed books are in good condition.
deeds.repository: Simpson County Clerk, Franklin; KDLA microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Kentucky land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — will books survive from county formation
wills_estates.gaps: Minor — some loose estate papers from the earliest decades are incomplete. Bound volumes are intact.

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation
marriages.gaps: Some early minister returns are incomplete. Marriage bonds are the more reliable source for the antebellum period.

court_minutes.completeness: Good — county court order books survive from formation and are continuous

vital_records.completeness: Kentucky mandated registration in 1852, but compliance in Simpson County was poor until approximately 1911. Tennessee's registration began in 1914 — so the border area has a gap in vital records on both sides.
vital_records.repository: Kentucky Office of Vital Statistics, Frankfort; KDLA for pre-1911 registers

substitute_sources.source_type: Allen, Logan, and Warren County parent records (pre-1819)
substitute_sources.description: Simpson County was formed from Allen, Logan, and Warren counties in 1819. All records before that date are in the parent counties. Logan's 1865 fire destroyed some early records; Warren County records are well-preserved.
substitute_sources.repository: Allen County Clerk, Scottsville; Logan County Clerk, Russellville; Warren County Clerk, Bowling Green; KDLA microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for families in the Franklin area before 1819. Warren County records (intact from 1797) are the most reliable substitute. Logan County records survive in fragments after the 1865 fire.
substitute_sources.source: Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1925.

substitute_sources.description: Simpson County sits directly on the Tennessee border. Robertson County (Springfield) and Sumner County (Gallatin) in Tennessee are the immediate neighbors. Families routinely crossed the state line for marriage, land purchase, church attendance, and commerce.
substitute_sources.repository: Robertson County Archives, Springfield, TN; Sumner County Archives, Gallatin, TN; Tennessee State Library and Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for any family in the Franklin area. The state line divided communities that functioned as single units. A family that appears to 'disappear' from Simpson County may have crossed into Robertson County, Tennessee, only a few miles away.
substitute_sources.source: Durham, Walter T. The Great Leap Westward: A History of Sumner County, Tennessee. Gallatin: Sumner County Public Library Board, 1969.

substitute_sources.description: The Louisville and Nashville Railroad ran through Franklin, making it a transportation hub. Railroad employment records, station records, and land acquisition files document families connected to the railroad.
substitute_sources.repository: CSX Transportation Archives (successor to L&N); University of Louisville Archives; Kentucky Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for families who worked for or were affected by the railroad. L&N land acquisition files document property purchases along the right-of-way.
substitute_sources.source: Klein, Maury. History of the Louisville & Nashville Railroad. New York: Macmillan, 1972.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1820-1940. Agricultural and slave schedules (1850-1880).
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential. Compare Simpson County and Robertson County, Tennessee census records side by side — families crossed the state line regularly.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Simpson County, KY.

narrative_hooks: Simpson County's courthouse has never burned, and {ancestor_name}'s records survive intact from 1819. If the trail goes cold, look south — the Tennessee border is a few miles away, and Robertson County may hold the next chapter.

The state line between Simpson County and Robertson County, Tennessee, divided communities that shared churches, stores, and family ties — {ancestor_name} may have crossed it so often that Kentucky and Tennessee records tell different parts of the same story.

Before Simpson County existed in 1819, {ancestor_name}'s records were filed in three parent counties — Allen, Logan, or Warren — depending on exactly where on the map the family lived, and the Logan County fire of 1865 complicates the trail for families on the western side.

The L&N Railroad ran through Franklin and turned Simpson County from a backroads border community into a transportation corridor — if {ancestor_name} was there when the railroad arrived, the family's economic world changed overnight.

Kentucky and Tennessee vital registration laws had different start dates — Kentucky 1852, Tennessee 1914 — so a family that moved across the state line may have no civil vital records for decades on either side.
source_references: Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1925.
Klein, Maury. History of the Louisville & Nashville Railroad. New York: Macmillan, 1972.
Durham, Walter T. The Great Leap Westward: A History of Sumner County, Tennessee. Gallatin: Sumner County Public Library Board, 1969.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Kentucky County Records. Frankfort, 1937-1942.
Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Records on Microfilm. Frankfort, various editions.
